title: The Song from the Kitchen
summary: A woman in Toronto hears an old song and is surprised that it brings back a whole place, not just a memory.
body:
Amara moved from Lagos to Toronto when she was nineteen. That was many years ago. Now she has a good job, warm friends, and a small flat near the lake. She does not feel sad about her new country. She likes it very much.

One winter evening, she was buying bread in a quiet shop. A song began to play from a small radio behind the counter. Amara stopped walking. She knew this song. Her mother used to sing it in the kitchen in Lagos, while she cooked rice and laughed with the neighbours.

Suddenly, Amara was not in the cold shop. She could almost smell the hot oil and the spices. She could feel the warm air on her arms. She could hear her mother's voice and the sound of the radio in their old house. For ten seconds, she was a child again, sitting on the kitchen floor.

Then the song ended, and she was back in Toronto, holding her bread. This is the strange power of a song. A photograph shows you a face, but a song can give you a whole room, a smell, and a feeling, all at the same time. Amara paid for her bread and walked home slowly. She was not sad. She was thankful. A small, simple song had kept her old kitchen safe inside her, for all these years, without her even knowing. Some things we save without trying.

annotations.phrase: the strange power of a song
annotations.meaningVi: sức mạnh kỳ lạ của một bài hát
mà cô thậm chí không hề biết

annotations.phrase: Some things we save without trying
annotations.meaningVi: Có những điều chúng ta giữ lại mà không cần cố gắng

grammarNotes.title: 'used to' for past habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'used to' để nói về thói quen trong quá khứ nay không còn nữa.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Past habit is shown with 'used to': 'Her mother used to sing it in the kitchen in Lagos.'

grammarNotes.title: Past continuous for background
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng quá khứ tiếp diễn để mô tả hành động đang diễn ra làm nền.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Past continuous sets the scene: 'she was buying bread in a quiet shop.'

grammarNotes.title: 'could' for ability and the senses
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'could' để diễn tả khả năng cảm nhận trong quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Sense ability uses 'could': 'She could almost smell the hot oil' and 'She could hear her mother's voice.'

translationVi:
Amara chuyển từ Lagos đến Toronto khi cô mười chín tuổi. Đó là nhiều năm trước. Bây giờ cô có một công việc tốt, những người bạn ấm áp, và một căn hộ nhỏ gần hồ. Cô không cảm thấy buồn về đất nước mới của mình. Cô rất thích nó.

Một buổi tối mùa đông, cô đang mua bánh mì trong một cửa hàng yên tĩnh. Một bài hát bắt đầu vang lên từ một chiếc radio nhỏ phía sau quầy. Amara dừng bước. Cô biết bài hát này. Mẹ cô đã từng hát nó trong bếp ở Lagos, trong khi bà nấu cơm và cười đùa với hàng xóm.

Đột nhiên, Amara không còn ở trong cửa hàng lạnh lẽo nữa. Cô gần như có thể ngửi thấy mùi dầu nóng và các loại gia vị. Cô có thể cảm nhận làn không khí ấm áp trên cánh tay mình. Cô có thể nghe thấy giọng của mẹ và tiếng radio trong ngôi nhà cũ của họ. Trong mười giây, cô lại là một đứa trẻ, ngồi trên sàn bếp.

Rồi bài hát kết thúc, và cô trở lại Toronto, tay cầm ổ bánh mì. Đây là sức mạnh kỳ lạ của một bài hát. Một bức ảnh cho bạn thấy một khuôn mặt, nhưng một bài hát có thể cho bạn cả một căn phòng, một mùi hương, và một cảm giác, tất cả cùng một lúc. Amara trả tiền bánh mì và đi bộ về nhà thật chậm. Cô không buồn. Cô biết ơn. Một bài hát nhỏ, đơn giản đã giữ căn bếp cũ của cô an toàn bên trong cô, suốt bao nhiêu năm qua, mà cô thậm chí không hề biết. Có những điều chúng ta giữ lại mà không cần cố gắng.

questions.explanation: She 'moved from Lagos to Toronto when she was nineteen'.

questions.prompt: How does Amara feel about her new country now?
questions.explanation: 'She does not feel sad about her new country. She likes it very much.'
questions.hint: Read the end of the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: Where was Amara when she heard the song?
options: In a quiet shop buying bread
questions.explanation: 'She was buying bread in a quiet shop' when the song played.

questions.prompt: Who used to sing that song?
questions.explanation: 'Her mother used to sing it in the kitchen in Lagos.'

questions.prompt: What did Amara almost smell when she heard the song?
options: The hot oil and the spices
questions.explanation: 'She could almost smell the hot oil and the spices.'

questions.prompt: For how long did Amara feel like a child again?
questions.explanation: 'For ten seconds, she was a child again.'
questions.hint: Look near the end of the third paragraph.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what can a song give you that a photograph cannot?
options: A whole room, a smell, and a feeling
questions.explanation: 'A photograph shows you a face, but a song can give you a whole room, a smell, and a feeling.'

questions.prompt: How did Amara feel after the song ended?
questions.explanation: 'She was not sad. She was thankful.'

questions.prompt: What does 'without her even knowing' suggest about the memory?
options: She chose to keep it on purpose
She kept it without trying or planning
questions.explanation: The song 'had kept her old kitchen safe inside her... without her even knowing', and 'Some things we save without trying.'
questions.hint: Look at the last two sentences.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Toronto is too cold for Amara
A song can bring back a whole place and feeling
Amara wants to move back to Lagos
questions.explanation: The text calls this 'the strange power of a song' to return a whole place.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the ending?
questions.explanation: She walked home slowly and was 'thankful', not sad.

cloze.template:
Amara moved from Lagos to Toronto when she was nineteen. That was many years ago. Now she has a good job, warm friends, and a small {{b1}} near the lake. She does not feel sad about her new country. She likes it very much.

One winter evening, she was buying {{b2}} in a quiet shop. A song began to play from a small radio behind the {{b3}}. Amara stopped walking. She knew this song. Her {{b4}} used to sing it in the kitchen in Lagos, while she cooked rice and laughed with the neighbours.

Suddenly, Amara was not in the cold shop. She could almost {{b5}} the hot oil and the spices. She could feel the warm air on her arms. She could {{b6}} her mother's voice and the sound of the radio in their old house. For ten {{b7}}, she was a child again, sitting on the kitchen floor.

Then the song ended, and she was back in Toronto, holding her bread. This is the strange {{b8}} of a song. A photograph shows you a face, but a song can give you a whole room, a smell, and a feeling, all at the same time. Amara paid for her bread and walked home slowly. She was not sad. She was {{b9}}. A small, simple song had kept her old kitchen {{b10}} inside her, for all these years, without her even knowing. Some things we {{b11}} without trying.

blanks.explanation: She has a 'small flat near the lake'.
She could 'almost smell the hot oil'.
'For ten seconds, she was a child again.'
'This is the strange power of a song.'
The song 'had kept her old kitchen safe inside her'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage says a song can give you 'a whole room, a smell, and a feeling' all at once. Is there one song or sound that brings back a specific place for you? What does it bring back, and why do you think it works that way?
